# Glycan Predictor {#glycan-predictor}

The Glycan Predictor module lets you look up any protein by its UniProt accession and view glycosylation-site predictions alongside known glycan associations from the knowledge graph. It brings together experimental annotations and machine-learning predictions in a single interface, making it straightforward to compare what is known with what the model infers.

## How to Use

Getting started is simple. Enter a UniProt ID (such as `P07602`) in the sidebar text input and click Look Up. Results appear across three tabs in the main panel: Protein Info, Site Predictions, and Known Glycans. Each tab offers a different lens on the protein's glycosylation profile.

```
<!-- screenshot placeholder: predictor_sidebar.png -->
<!-- Sidebar with UniProt ID input showing "P07602" and the Look Up button. -->
```

## Protein Info

The first tab presents a metadata card summarising key annotations pulled from the knowledge graph. You will see the gene symbol, organism, protein name, the total number of glycosylation sites recorded in the KG, and whether ESM-2 embeddings are available for this protein. Below the card, a DataTable lists all glycosylation sites annotated for the protein, showing each site's amino-acid position, residue type, full site identifier (e.g. `SITE::P07602::187::N`), and the evidence source such as UniProt, GlyGen, or PTMCode. Sorting by position reveals the sites in sequence order, and clicking any row highlights that site in the Predictions tab.

```
<!-- screenshot placeholder: protein_info_card.png -->
<!-- The metadata card for P07602 (prosaposin) with gene, organism, and site count. -->
```

## Site Predictions

The second tab is where the predictive modelling comes to life. It displays pre-computed N-linked glycosylation site predictions generated by the glycoMusubi prediction pipeline.

The prediction workflow starts with ESM-2 embeddings: the `esm2_t33_650M_UR50D` model extracts a 1,280-dimensional embedding vector for each residue in the protein. These per-residue vectors are then fed into a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) trained to classify each asparagine within an N-X-S/T sequon as glycosylated or not. The MLP outputs a probability score between 0 and 1, and predictions are grouped into clusters based on sequence context similarity.

```
<!-- screenshot placeholder: prediction_pipeline_diagram.png -->
<!-- Flow diagram: Protein sequence -> ESM-2 -> per-residue embeddings ->
     MLP classifier -> site-level probabilities. -->
```

The predictions table shows each candidate sequon's position, the N-X-S/T motif, the model's probability score, a binary yes/no prediction (using a default threshold of 0.5), whether the site is experimentally annotated, and the cluster assignment. Probability values at or above 0.8 indicate high confidence that the site is glycosylated. Scores between 0.5 and 0.8 represent moderate confidence and are worth investigating experimentally. Scores below 0.5 suggest the site is unlikely to be glycosylated. Depending on your use case, you might mentally adjust the threshold: use 0.3 or above for high recall (catching all possible sites), or restrict to 0.8 and above for high precision (minimising false positives).

```
<!-- screenshot placeholder: site_predictions_table.png -->
<!-- DT table for P07602 showing 12 candidate sequons, 5 predicted as glycosylated,
     probability column colour-coded from red (low) to green (high). -->
```

The underlying model achieves strong performance on the held-out test set, with an F1 score of 0.924, precision of 0.931, recall of 0.917, and AUROC of 0.968. In practical terms, for every 100 predictions roughly 93 positives are correct and roughly 92 true sites are detected. Keep in mind that these are averages across all test proteins; performance may vary for individual proteins, especially those with unusual glycosylation patterns or limited homologs in the training data.

## Known Glycans

The third tab lists all glycans associated with the protein in the knowledge graph, connected via `has_glycan` edges. Each row shows the GlyTouCan accession, monosaccharide composition string, molecular mass in Daltons, glycan subclass category, and the data source (GlyGen or GlyTouCan). Clicking a Glycan ID opens the corresponding GlyTouCan entry in a new browser tab when external links are enabled.

```
<!-- screenshot placeholder: known_glycans_table.png -->
<!-- DT table showing 23 glycans associated with P07602, sorted by mass. -->
```

## Limitations

There are several important caveats to keep in mind when using this module. On the coverage front, predictions are only available for proteins that have pre-computed ESM-2 embeddings. Currently 1,611 of the 9,138 proteins in the KG have embeddings and predictions; entering a protein without them will produce an informational message rather than a predictions table.

In terms of scope, the model predicts N-linked glycosylation only -- O-linked and other modification types are not covered. Predictions are based entirely on sequence information via ESM-2, so they do not account for tissue-specific expression or cellular localisation. Two edge cases deserve mention: if a protein has multiple isoforms, site positions refer to the canonical sequence and may differ on alternative isoforms; and very long proteins (over 2,000 residues) may have lower prediction accuracy due to ESM-2 context window limits.

## Worked Example: Prosaposin (P07602)

Prosaposin is a well-characterised glycoprotein with multiple N-linked glycosylation sites, making it an ideal test case. Enter `P07602` and click Look Up. The Protein Info tab shows the gene symbol PSAP, organism *Homo sapiens*, and 5 known glycosylation sites. Switching to Site Predictions reveals 12 candidate sequons, five of which have probability above 0.5 and match the known annotations -- a reassuring confirmation of the model's accuracy on this protein. The Known Glycans tab displays 23 glycan structures associated with prosaposin in the KG.

```
<!-- screenshot placeholder: p07602_example_full.png -->
<!-- Three-tab view for P07602 showing the protein info card, predictions table
     with 5 highlighted true positives, and known glycans list. -->
```

## Next Steps

To go beyond individual site predictions and explore how well the system can identify the correct glycan for a given protein, continue to Chapter \@ref(glycan-retrieval).
